coredata.dc:title: Identification of fluorescent nanoparticles from roasted sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) during normal cooking procedures
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Bin
Cui, Guoxin
Wang, Haitao
Song, Yukun
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nanoparticles (FNs) were identified from roasted sweet potato with a particle size of 7.0, 2.8, 2.4 nm for peel FNs, and 2.3, 1.9, 2.2 nm for pulp FNs after roasting at 250 °C for 20, 40, and 60 min, respectively. Blue fluorescence of FNs was observed under ultra-violet light excitation. The fluorescence intensity of the FNs increased when they were transferred from acidic to alkaline environment, and the FNs showed good photostability. The fluorescence of peel and pulp FNs was quenched 42.65% and 35.59%, respectively, in vitro digestion. The FNs could be absorbed by the digestive tracts of mice and enter liver, heart, kidney, brain, lung and testis. A distinct interaction between FNs and dopamine was confirmed in weakly alkaline aqueous solution. This work provided useful information in understanding the property of FNs generated in plant-based food of roasted sweet potato during normal cooking.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanoparticles
Sweet potato
Roasting
Digestion
Interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Identification of FNs from roasted sweet potato
Characterization of the FNs from roasted sweet potato

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Central Funds Guiding the Local Science and Technology Development
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31872915 ) and the Central Funds Guiding the Local Science and Technology Development ( 2020JH6/10500002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent nanoparticles (FNs) derived from roasted sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) during normal cooking procedures (upper schematic illustration). TEM images of FNs from the sweet potato peel roasted for (A) 20 min, (E) 40 min, (I) 60 min or (M) boiled for 60 min. Corresponding size of FNs from peel roasted for (B) 20 min, (E) 40 min, (J) 60 min, or (N) from pulp boiled for 60 min; TEM images of FNs from sweet potato pulp roasted for (C) 20 min, (G) 40 min, (K) 60 min, or (O) from pulp boiled for 60 min. Corresponding size of FNs from pulp roasted for (D) 20 min, (H) 40 min, (L) 60 min, or (P) from pulp boiled for 60 min.

Photographs of FN aqueous solution from (A) peel and (B) pulp of sweet potato before (raw) and after being roasted for 20, 40 and 60 min, or boiled for 60 min; Fluorescent photographs of FN aqueous solution from (C) peel and (D) pulp of sweet potato before (raw) and after being roasted for 20, 40 and 60 min, or boiled for 60 min. The extracts from peel and pulp do not show fluorescence (raw). FT-IR spectra of (E) peel FNs and (F) peel FNs before and after being roasted for 20, 40 and 60 min, or boiled for 60 min.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescent (FL) emission spectra of FNs from peel of the sweet potato roasted for (A) 20 min, (B) 40 min, (C) 60 min, and (D) boiled for 60 min. Decay curves of FNs from peel of the sweet potato roasted for (E) 20 min, (F) 40 min, (G) 60 min, and (H) boiled for 60 min. UV–vis absorption and fluorescent FL emission spectra of FNs from pulp of the sweet potato roasted for (I) 20 min, (J) 40 min, (K) 60 min, and (L) boiled for 60 min. Decay curves of FNs from pulp of the sweet potato roasted for (M) 20 min, (N) 40 min, (O) 60 min, and (P) boiled for 60 min.
XPS spectrum of (A) peel FNs and (B) pulp FNs, High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of (C) peel FNs, and (D) pulp FNs. High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of (E) peel FNs, and (F) pulp FNs.

Fluorescent images of mice organs before and after oral administration of peel and peel FNs at 0.5, 2, 6 and 24 h. The FNs were obtained from sweet potato roasted for 60 min. Histograms show the relative fluorescence intensity of each organ before and after oral administration of FNs for 0.5, 2, 6 and 24 h in mice (n = 3).

High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s from the (A) peel FN-DA, and (B) pulp FN-DA. Fluorescent emission spectra of (C) peel FN-DA, and (D) pulp FN-DA. Photographs of peel FNs, peel FN-DA, pulp FNs and pulp FN-DA aqueous solution under (E) daylight and (F) excitation of 365 nm UV light.

head.ce:title: Identification of fluorescent nanoparticles from roasted sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) during normal cooking procedures

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi district, Dalian, 116034, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nanoparticles (FNs) were identified from roasted sweet potato with a particle size of 7.0, 2.8, 2.4 nm for peel FNs, and 2.3, 1.9, 2.2 nm for pulp FNs after roasting at 250 °C for 20, 40, and 60 min, respectively. Blue fluorescence of FNs was observed under ultra-violet light excitation. The fluorescence intensity of the FNs increased when they were transferred from acidic to alkaline environment, and the FNs showed good photostability. The fluorescence of peel and pulp FNs was quenched 42.65% and 35.59%, respectively, in vitro digestion. The FNs could be absorbed by the digestive tracts of mice and enter liver, heart, kidney, brain, lung and testis. A distinct interaction between FNs and dopamine was confirmed in weakly alkaline aqueous solution. This work provided useful information in understanding the property of FNs generated in plant-based food of roasted sweet potato during normal cooking.

ce:section.ce:para: Sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) is known as one of the nutritional tuber crops which contains minerals, Vitamin C, dietary fiber and some antioxidants such as beta-carotene and anthocyanin (Kim et al., 2012; Teow et al., 2007). Meanwhile, roasted sweet potatoes were consumed broadly around the world as a snack or side dish. It is attributable that roasting could encourage the conversion of sweet potato from dense to the soft texture and bring out the sweetness. However, in recent years, people become more concerned about the health issue in roasted food. Previous studies have verified that the food process, such as roasting, grilling, and baking, could result in the production of acrylamides (Balagiannis et al., 2019), polycyclic aromatics hydrocarbons (Houessou, Goujot, Heyd, & Camel, 2008) which are positively related to the occurrence of cancers (Hogervorst, van den Brandt, Godschalk, van Schooten, & Schouten, 2017; Kotemori et al., 2018). However, except for the small molecular compounds, the effects of those macromolecular structures, such as nanoparticles, produced during the roasting process might be neglected. Therefore, the study of foodborne nanoparticles (FNs) production during food thermal processing and exploring their potential effects for human health has received considerable attention.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of ultra-small nano-materials with a size generally less than 10 nm, which have excellent luminescent properties, good water solubility, easy chemical modification, and functional integration (Wang et al., 2019). The CDs can be formed through bottom-up method (Liu, Wu, Feng, & Mullen, 2011) or top-down approach (Tan et al., 2013). As for the top-down approach, it could be hypothesized that the foodborne fluorescent nanoparticles (FNs), similar to CDs, may be formed during cooking process. For instance, previous studies have proved that the FNs could be produced during thermal treatment of foods, such as grilled beef hamburger, roasted pike ell, and roasted lamb (Bi et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019). Similarly, several reports showed that the FNs could be found through commercial products such as coffee, kvass, and Coca-Cola (Jiang et al., 2014; Li, Jiang, Wang, Cong, & Tan, 2018; Liao et al., 2015). The bio-molecules from foods could provide plenty of carbohydrates containing C, O, and H elements, which may act as raw materials for the formation of FNs. It is reasonable to assume that the FNs could generate from roasted sweet potato, and the produced FNs might enter the human body along with the consuming and have complex interaction after oral uptake. As a bio-active molecule, dopamine (DA) is belonging to the catecholamine and phenethylamine families that synthesized in the human brain and kidney (Felder, Felder, Eisner, & Jose, 1989). Lacking DA in brains may be related to Parkinson's disease (Ferrari et al., 2016). Research showed that external stimulus such as ammonium persulfate might work as a trigger for the polymerization of DA (Bernsmann et al., 2011). However, it is unknown how the FNs interact with DA in vitro as the FNs may be eaten-up with roasted sweet potato. Our hypothesis is that the FNs have the ultra-small size and may pass through the blood-brain barrier, thus interacting with DA and causing uncertain effects.

In this work, the identification of FNs from roasted sweet potato during cooking process was reported, and their composition and structural characteristics were thoroughly analyzed. The size and morphology of these FNs characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and their functional groups were characterized with Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR). The composition assessed with the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and their optical properties evaluated with fluorescent spectroscopy. The FNs showed good stability in artificial digestive juices. In vivo biodistribution within various organs was evaluated by oral administration of these FNs into mice. Moreover, in vitro interaction between the FNs with DA was determined by investigation their ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorbance and fluorescence change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of FNs from roasted sweet potato
ce:section.ce:para: Sweet potatoes about 150–300 g purchased from Jingdong market, Liuao peninsula, Zhangpu County, Fujian, China. The sweet potatoes were washed carefully with deionized water to clean the impurities on the surface and put into the electric oven and roasted at 250 °C for 20, 40 and 60 min, respectively. Besides, the washed sweet potatoes were put in boiling water and cooked for 60 min as a control. Then the roasted or boiled sweet potato peel and inner pulp were collected, respectively, and mixed with 1000 mL of 100% ethanol, stirred at room temperature for 24 h. After removing the solid residues by vacuum filtration, the ethanol evaporated through rotary evaporation and the raw products were dissolved in deionized water. Continually, liposoluble constituents were removed by extraction with ethyl acetate. Subsequently, the water phase was collected and filtered through a dialysis bag with molecular weight cutoff 3500 Da against deionized water for two days. The dialysis solution was collected, lyophilized, and stored at 4 °C as a powder for further characterization and evaluation.

JEM-2100 (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) transmission electron microscope used for TEM imaging. Fluorescence spectra obtained by using fluorescence spectrometer (F-2700, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (Lambda 35, PerkinElmer, Cambridge, MA, U.S.A.) used at room temperature for absorption spectra measurements. FT-IR spectra recorded in the KBr medium by using FT-IR infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Norwalk, CT, USA). XPS spectra characterized the chemical compositions through ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo VG, Waltham, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns measured by diffractometer (XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence lifetimes measured by FLS980 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments Co. UK). In vivo imaging was performed by a MIIS system (Molecular Device Corporation, Sunnyvale, CA, USA).
The details of experiment methods can be found in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the FNs from roasted sweet potato
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we came across the presence of FNs in roasted sweet potato when the extractant of ethanol solution showed blue fluorescence under UV light. The FNs were separately collected from sweet potato peel and pulp, and their morphology was characterized. Fig. 1 A, E, 1I show the TEM images of the FNs from sweet potato peel after roasting for 20, 40 and 60 min. Their corresponding average size is 7.0, 2.8, 2.4 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1B, F and 1J. The FNs obtained after 20 min roasting exhibit a polymer-like morphology which have no regular structures and show as clusters in an aggregation state. With the increase of roasting time, the FN size decreased, and the nanoparticles become compacted. In contrast, the FNs from pulp roasted for 20 min have aggregation and cross-linking with each other. The average size of small particles is about 2.3 nm (Fig. 1C and D). When the roasting time increased to 40 min, the size of FNs from pulp decreased to around 1.9 nm (Fig. 1H), while no cross-linking among the FNs found and the particles were well mono-dispersed (Fig. 1G). As the roasting time reached 60 min, the FNs from pulp became more homogenous (Fig. 1K), and the size was approximately 1.5–3.2 nm with a maximum population at 2.2 nm (Fig. 1L). In a word, the FNs can be produced in both sweet potato peel and pulp during roasting for 20–60 min, and the FNs changed from polymer-like particle to compact dots with the extension of roasting time. In addition, the control samples such as raw sweet potato also investigated, and no FNs found before roasting (data not shown). However, the sweet potato cooked by boiling water, as displayed in Fig. 1M and O also had FNs. The average size of FNs from peel and pulp of sweet potato after boiling for 60 min was 6.0 and 1.7 nm, respectively. This demonstrated that the FNs were not present in the raw sweet potato, and they formed in both roasting and boiling processes. It was reported that the fluorescent CDs could be produced from orange juice after hydrothermal reaction (Sahu, Behera, Maiti, & Mohapatra, 2012). While, in this study, we confirmed that the fluorescent FNs were produced in regular cooking of sweet potato. The fluorescent CDs were also isolated from instant coffee after treatment with hot water at 90 °C (Jiang et al., 2014). This indicated that the CDs could be ubiquitously present in plant-based food material.

Fig. 2 shows the photographs of FNs aqueous solution from peel and pulp of sweet potato before (raw) and after being roasted for 20, 40 and 60 min, or boiled for 60 min. The FN solution is transparent, and no turbidity observed (Fig. 2 A and B), indicating that the FNs have good water-solubility. The fluorescent photographs of FNs (Fig. 2 C and D) reveal that the FNs from cooked sweet potato are fluorescent, while the extracts from peel and pulp do not show fluorescence. Moreover, FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to identify the surface chemical characteristics of the two types of FNs. The result in Fig. 2E shows a broad peak at 3390 cm−1 that can be ascribed to the O–H stretching vibration (Bi et al., 2018). The peak at 2931 cm−1 was assigned to the C–H stretching vibration in FNs (Song et al., 2017). The bands at 1635 cm−1 corresponded to the CO stretching and N–H bending and the peak at 1417 cm−1 was due to the scissoring vibration of CC (Zhou, Sheng, Han, Zou, & Li, 2012). Correspondingly, an absorption band at 1048 cm−1 indicated the presence of C–O stretching vibration (Bi et al., 2018). With the extension of roasting time, the absorbance peaks become stronger. Likewise, the FNs from pulp show similar peak position and trends of FT-IR peaks (Fig. 2F). For the FNs derived from peel and pulp in the boiled sweet potato, they have not only the above-mentioned peaks but also the peaks of stretching vibration of CO at 1,773, 1716 cm−1, possibly due to the hydration of heating medium water (Shen, Shang, Chen, Wang, & Cai, 2017). All the results of the FT-IR analysis showed that there are plenty of hydroxyl, carbonyl/carboxyl groups on the surface of the FNs.

Optical properties are of importance in the evaluation of the sweet potato FNs. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission spectra of FNs from sweet potato are shown in Fig. 3 . The FNs from peel, whether roasting or boiling, have a maximum absorbance at 252 nm with a shoulder at 287 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* electron transition at carbon π bonds of CC band (Mehta, Jha, Basu, Singhal, & Kailasa, 2015). The maximum absorbance band of FNs from pulp varied between 252 and 287 nm. Also, the FL emission profile of the FNs from peel and pulp shows a cooking time-dependent behaviour. The peaks are incomplete and change stronger with the extension of cooking time. The maximum emission wavelength increased from 415 to 440 nm (Fig. 3 A, B, C) for peel FNs, and from 420 to 460 nm (Fig. 3 I, J, K) for pulp FNs. The maximum emission wavelength for FNs from peel and pulp in sweet potato after 60 boiling is 460 nm (Fig. 3 D, L). It was due to the distribution of different size particles had different energy levels associated with multiple functional groups on the FN surface (Jia, Li, & Wang, 2012).

The excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviour observed with a redshift toward higher excitation. The reason for the redshift of fluorescence emission is similar to the artificially prepared CDs (Mehta et al., 2015). The QY of peel and pulp FNs from the roasted sweet potato listed in Table S1 exhibit an increasing trend with the extension of cooking time using quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 (λex = 340 nm) as a reference. With the same roasting time, the QY of peel FNs is larger than that of pulp FNs. In contrast, the QY of peel FNs is smaller than that of pulp FNs for the boiled sweet potato. The higher oxidation degree of peel FNs compared with that of pulp FNs might be relevant to the open-field exposure to oxygen during roasting. The higher oxidation level of FNs could produce more surface states, thus leading to the FL emission with diverse energy levels at different excitations (Long et al., 2012). Meanwhile, the cooking water probably resulted in a decrease of the peel FNs in boiled sweet potato. The FL decay can fitted with a two-exponential equation, and the fluorescence lifetime of peel and pulp FNs was measured to be 3.08 and 5.06 ns (Fig. 3 E, F, G, H, M, N, O, P). The short lifetimes are quite similar to that of CDs which have the radiative recombination nature of excitations (Zhou et al., 2012).

Taking the FNs produced from sweet potato after roasting for 60 min as examples (both here and below), their elemental composition was further characterized by XPS spectroscopy. XPS survey indicates three peaks at 285, 398.4, and 531.8 eV from the peel and pulp FN that can be assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, with a composition ratio of carbon 71.49%, nitrogen 0.9%, and oxygen 27.44% (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4C shows the high-resolution spectra of C1s, and the peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.4, and 288.2 eV were assigned for CC, C–O/C–N, CO, and O–CO, respectively (-Li et al., 2017). The O1s high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 4E) around 531.5 and 531.9 eV indicate the existance of CO and O–CO, respectively (Liao et al., 2015). Analogous results were found in FNs from the pulp (Fig. 4B), with the atomic ratio of C, N to O of 78.63%, 0.64%, and 20.74%. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s can be divided into four peaks (Fig. 4D). The peaks positions at 284.8, 286.2, 287.4 and 288.2 eV are similar to those of peel FNs, responding to CC, C–O/C–N, CO, and OC bonds, respectively (Liao et al., 2015). The high-resolution spectra of O1s (Fig. 4F) show two significant peaks at 531.5, 531.9 eV for CO and O–CO. The results of XPS analysis agreed well with those of FT-IR spectroscopy, showing that there are hydroxy and carbonyl/carboxyl groups on the surfaces with sp2 and sp3 hybrid structures. Consistently, the XPS results indicated the availability of nitrogen element from roasted sweet potato peel and pulp. This is consistent with the artificially synthesized CDs derived from watermelon peel (Zhou et al., 2012) and apple juice (Mehta et al., 2015). It can be explained that the carbohydrate in sweet potato become denaturation, precipitated, leading to the production of the FNs after high-temperature roasting. Moreover, Fig. S1 shows that the XRD spectrum of peel FNs (roasting for 60 min) has a broad peak centred around 21.84° and a peak at 22.98° for pulp FNs (roasting for 60 min), suggesting the amorphous nature of these two types of FNs have a similar structure. This is the first report that the FNs can be produced in sweet potato during the regular cooking process, instead of the preparation procedure in a harsh condition like hydrothermal reaction.

The stability of fluorescence is an essential factor in the evaluation of sweet potato FNs. Both peel and pulp FNs show a pH-dependent fluorescence emission. As shown in Figs. S2A and 2B, the FNs from both peel and pulp display relatively stable emission in the acidic environment. Additionally, as pH increased from 6 to 11, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually under alkaline condition. Under alkaline environment, the carboxyl groups existed as carboxylate with the allusion of carboxyl groups on the FNs surface (Fang et al., 2018). The lower pH values of FNs could make these carboxylates more protonated and weaken the repulsive force, leading to aggregation of FNs and fluorescence attenuation under acid environment. The photostability of FNs was investigated by continuous irradiation under 365 nm UV-light for 80 min. Figs. S2C and 2D show the fluorescence intensity of peel FNs decreased quicker than pulp FNs with the irradiation time extension. This result indicated that the surface molecular groups were strongly affected by the UV radiation and the pulp FNs showed better photostability against photobleaching than peel FNs.

In vitro biological activity of the FNs should be relevant to the human consumption of sweet potato, for example, during digestion in the gastrointestinal tract, the FNs might be degraded/digested. The digestion of FNs in the gastrointestinal tract simulated with the artificial saliva, gastric and small intestinal juice through the fluorescence quenching (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity was significantly influenced by the different digestion environment, suggesting the degradation of the FNs. When added to the saliva juice and digested for 5 min, the fluorescence intensity of FNs from peel and pulp only decreased by 3.59% and 0.15%, respectively. After 2 h incubation with gastric juice, the fluorescence intensity decreased 25.53% and 30.19%, respectively, for the FNs from peel and pulp. This demonstrated that the pulp FNs were more stable against degradation of gastric juice. Moreover, after digestion with small intestinal juice for 2 h, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the FNs from peel and pulp was further reduced by 13.53% and 5.25%, respectively. In summary, the artificial digestion juice resulted in more degradation of the peel FNs as compared with pulp FNs, which was consistent with the UV-irradiation result (Fig. S2). The results indicated that the FNs would be degraded or digested during digestion in the gastrointestinal tract when human consume sweet potato.

In vivo biodistribution of the FNs from peel and pulp of sweet potato roasted for 60 min was then studied by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticles after incubation with artificial digestive juices. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescent intensity of the most organs increased after oral administration of the FNs by mice. As compared with that of the control organs, suggesting that the FNs entered the body of the mice. Notably, the fluorescent intensity within stomach, intestine, liver and heart showed the maximum value at 0.5 h after administration, while 2 h for kidney and brain, and 6 h for lung. As for the testis, the fluorescence intensity reached its maximum value at 24 h.

There is no significant difference between the peel FNs and pulp FNs. The dynamic biodistribution of the FNs revealed that the FNs from the roasted sweet potato could distribute within the tested organs, even the brain and testis. The phenomenon of FNs observed in the brain might be due to the FNs uptake by the reticuloendothelial system (Yuan et al., 2009), and it is probably due to their small size, good hydrophilicity and penetration ability (Cox et al., 2018). However, their long-term effect on the human body is uncertain, and it is a research topic, whether we worry about them or not.

The potential biological effects of food-borne nanoparticles have drawn attention due to their unique structure and physicochemical properties. DA plays critical roles in the signal transduction in the brain, and since the FNs were confirmed that they could enter mice brain, so we hypothesized that the FNs from the roasted sweet potato might interact with DA under specific circumstance. The interaction of nanoparticles with dopamine would influence the activity of dopamine, and dysfunction of DA in brains may be related to Parkinson's disease (Ferrari et al., 2016). This has caused our interest in studying their interaction if the FNs in roasted sweet potato eaten up with sweet potato. For this purpose, in vitro reaction between the sweet potato FNs and DA was assessed.

The morphology changes of FNs before and after interaction with DA investigated by TEM. Figs. S4A, S4B, and S4C show the TEM images of the PDA, peel FN-DA, and pulp FN-DA nanoparticles, with an average size of 5.30 ± 1.2 (Fig. S4D), 3.85 ± 1.5 (Fig. S4E) and 3.13 ± 1.1 nm (Fig. S4F), respectively. Compared with the particle size of peel FNs and pulp FNs, the size of FNs (Fig. 1) significantly increased after interaction with DA to form enlarged conjugates. Meanwhile, the formed peel FN-DA and pulp FN-DA conjugates are relatively smaller than PDA. This indicated that the sweet potato FNs were able to interact with DA under the alkaline environment with pH 8.5.

The UV–vis absorption spectra the peel FNs, PDA, peel FN-DA and pulp FNs, PDA, pulp FN-DA shows in Fig. S5A, and Fig. S5B, respectively. A distinct absorption peak at 282 nm and a broad peak at 400 nm observed for PDA polymer, and two absorption peaks of peel FN-DA were found at 281 and 223 nm, respectively. In addition, the absorption peak of pulp FN-DA occurred at 282 and 222 nm. Compared with the UV–vis absorption peak at 287 nm from peel and pulp FNs, significant peak shifting was found, and a new peak appeared, suggesting the formation of new conjugates after mixing the FNs and DA. This could be evidence to support that FNs interacted with DA under specific circumstance.

The FT-IR spectroscopy peak positions of peel FN-DA (Fig. S5C) and pulp FN-DA (Fig. S5D) show significant change as a comparison with those of PDA spectrum. The peak present at 3193 cm−1 assigned to the absorbance of amino groups of PDAs. The peaks at 1611–1622 cm−1 are assigned to CO stretching band, and the peaks at 1519 cm−1 and 1531 cm−1 identified as CN stretching vibration, possibly due to the Schiff base reaction that formative imine with hydroxyl group (LaVoie, Ostaszewski, Weihofen, Schlossmacher, & Selkoe, 2005). The peaks at 2069–2070 cm−1 could be known as N–H bending vibration. Correspondingly, the new middle peak present at 1059 cm−1 could be contributed to C–N form Schiff base. These absorption peaks displayed the combined profile for peel, pulp FNs, and PDA after forming conjugates of FNs-DA.

XPS spectroscopy used to explain the interaction of FNs and DA. The N content of peel FN-DA was 5.53% (Fig. S5E) and 3.25% in pulp FN-DA (Fig. S5F) increased as compared with those of the peel FNs (N 0.90%) and pulp FNs (N 0.64%) as mentioned above (Fig. 4), also lower than the 7.04% of N content in PDA (Fig. S7A). This demonstrated the attachment of DA onto the sweet potato FNs. Compared with PDA C1s (Fig. S6B) and O1s (Fig. S6C) XPS spectra, the change of surface compounds in C1s spectra of peel FN-DA (Fig. 6 A) and pulp FN-DA spectra (Fig. 6B) could be attributed to the hydroxyl group in FNs react with the amino group of DA (LaVoie et al., 2005). As confirmed by the FT-IR spectra, the hydroxyl groups act as strong hydrogen receptor that can inhibit the formation of PDA (Zhang, Thingholm, Goldie, Ogaki, & Stadler, 2012), and lead to obvious size decrease of FNs-DA conjugates.

Fig. 6C and D shows the maximum emission wavelength for peel FN-DA conjugation at 456 nm, and the pulp FN-DA at 492 nm. These two types of FNs-DA conjugates both exhibited redshift on their FL spectra compared with the original FNs, which verified that there was an interaction between FNs and DA. Moreover, the redshift degree of peel FN-DA was less than pulp FN-DA due to less polar groups on the FNs outward (Zhu, Tang, Zhang, & Yang, 2012). Meanwhile, the colour of FNs-DA aqueous solution was brown under daylight (Fig. 6E), and the fluorescence colour changed from blue to green under 365 nm UV-light (Fig. 6F). The fluorescence colour change agreed with the fluorescence spectra. It can attribute to the Schiff base formation reaction after DA addition into the FNs.

Furthermore, the measured fluorescence lifetime of peel FN-DA (Fig. S8A) and pulp FN-DA (Fig. S8B) is 3.94 ns and 3.50 ns, receptively, which decreased 15% and 27% compared with those before interaction with DA. However, it was still longer than the PDA lifetime of 3.03 ns (Fig. S8C). This phenomenon was due to fluorescence quenching causing by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from FNs to DA. The electron excitation energy transferred between the energy donner of FNs and accepter DA, which was confirmed by dynamic fluorescence decay through the fluorescence lifetime change. Also, the non-radiative decay process of intramolecular energy transfer might occur because of the interaction of DA and FNs surface functional groups (Bala et al., 2018).

This study demonstrated the identification of FNs in roasted sweet potato, a plant food, during the normal roasting instead of using harsh reaction condition. The FNs from sweet potato peel and pulp possessed different particle sizes, and their particle size decreased with the extension of roasting time. These two types of FNs exhibited good photostability under the UV-light irradiation, and they could enter the major organs of mice after oral administration. In vitro experiment suggested that the FNs can react with DA. This finding revealed that foodborne nanoparticles could generate from the plant base food material during normal cooking and provided new information for the potential utilization of the FNs.

Bin Lai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Guoxin Cui: Data curation. Haitao Wang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Yukun Song: Writing - review & editing. Mingqian Tan: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All the authors did not have any conflicts of interest. All the authors agreed to submit the report for publication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915) and the Central Funds Guiding the Local Science and Technology Development (2020JH6/10500002).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lwt.2020.109989.
